title: Going to â€” review and deepen
description: Master 'going to' for decided plans and predictions based on present evidence. Build on A1 knowledge with more complex uses.

content.title: Going to â€” two main uses
content.body:
GOING TO has two important uses:

1. DECIDED PLANS (you already decided before speaking)
â€¢ I'm going to apply for a new job this year.
â€¢ We're going to repaint the living room next month.
â€¢ She's going to study medicine.

2. PREDICTIONS based on evidence (you can see something now)
â€¢ Look at those clouds â€” it's going to rain!
â€¢ He's not wearing a helmet â€” he's going to fall!
â€¢ The traffic is terrible â€” we're going to be late.
content.grammar_note:
am/is/are + going to + base verb
Evidence predictions: you can SEE the reason right now

content.title: Going to negative and questions
content.body:
Negative: am/is/are NOT going to + base verb
â€¢ I'm not going to waste my time on this.
â€¢ She isn't going to join that company.
â€¢ They aren't going to make it on time.

Questions: Am/Is/Are + subject + going to + base verb?
â€¢ Are you going to attend the training?
â€¢ What are you going to do after college?
â€¢ Is she going to accept the offer?

Short answers:
â€¢ Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
â€¢ Yes, she is. / No, she isn't.
content.grammar_note:
Negative: NOT going to + base verb
Question: Are you going to + base verb?

vocabulary.example: I intend to finish this course by December.
We're aiming to launch the app next quarter.
She's going to apply for the MBA programme.
He's going to resign from his current position.

vocabulary.meaning: to move something to a later time
vocabulary.example: They're going to postpone the event.
I'm definitely going to save more this year.

sentences: I'm going to start learning Python next month.
Look at those clouds â€” it's going to pour!
Are you going to apply for the promotion?
She isn't going to renew her contract.
They're going to build a new metro line in this area.

content.question: Arjun has decided to quit his job and start his own business. Which sentence is correct?
options: Arjun will quit his job tomorrow.
Arjun is going to start his own business.
Arjun is starting his own business tomorrow.
content.correct: Arjun is going to start his own business.
content.explanation: A decision already made before speaking = GOING TO. (Note: present continuous would also be possible if a date was arranged, but GOING TO is more natural for a decision without a fixed date.)

content.question: You see your friend walking fast toward a wet floor without noticing the warning sign. What do you shout?
content.explanation: You can see the evidence right now â€” wet floor + he's not stopping = prediction based on evidence â†’ GOING TO.

content.question: Which sentence shows a DECIDED PLAN using 'going to'?
options: I think it might rain later.
I'm going to visit my grandparents during Diwali â€” I already bought the ticket.
Look â€” she's going to drop that tray!
I will help you if you need me.
content.correct: I'm going to visit my grandparents during Diwali â€” I already bought the ticket.
content.explanation: A pre-decided plan = GOING TO. The other options are prediction (might), evidence prediction, or will-offer.

content.question: Kavya says: 'The baby looks really tired â€” she ___ fall asleep soon.' Which fits?
content.explanation: Visible evidence (baby looks tired) â†’ prediction with GOING TO.

content.instruction: Fill in with the correct 'going to' form.

sentences.text: My brother ___ (go) to the USA next year for his Masters degree â€” he's already applied.
I ___ (not/join) that gym â€” it's too expensive.
Look at that car â€” it ___ (crash)! It's going too fast.
___ you ___ (tell) her the news today? (Are/going to)

content.instruction: Is it a PLAN or EVIDENCE PREDICTION? Fill in 'going to' and label each.

sentences.text: She already handed in her resignation â€” she ___ leave the company. [type?]
The traffic on OMR is terrible â€” we ___ be very late. [type?]
sentences.answer: are going to â€” EVIDENCE PREDICTION

sentences.text: He's decided to propose to her this weekend â€” he ___ buy a ring today. [type?]

content.instruction: Arrange the words into a correct sentence.
content.answer: We're going to renovate our house next year.

content.instruction: Arrange into an evidence prediction sentence.
content.answer: Watch â€” he's going to fall on that step!

content.instruction: Match the situation to the correct GOING TO sentence.

pairs.left: She booked a flight to London last month.
pairs.right: She's going to fly to London.

pairs.left: The ice cream is melting fast in the sun.
He signed up for a gym membership yesterday.
pairs.right: He's going to start working out.

pairs.left: The queue is enormous â€” 200 people!
pairs.right: We're going to wait a very long time.

pairs.left: They decided not to continue the project.
pairs.right: They aren't going to continue the project.

content.title: New Year resolutions â€” Chennai
content.setting: Priya and her friend Arjun are talking about their plans and resolutions for the coming year.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Priya, it's almost the new year! Do you have any big plans or resolutions?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes, quite a few! I'm going to start a savings plan â€” I'm going to put aside 20% of my salary every month.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: That's very disciplined! I've been meaning to do that too. Any other plans?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm going to apply for a certification course in data analytics. It starts in February.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Wow, you're serious! What about fitness? Are you going to join a gym or something?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm not going to join a gym â€” it didn't work for me last time. But I'm going to start running in the mornings at Besant Nagar beach.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: That's a great idea! What about travel? Are you going to go anywhere?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes! My cousin and I are going to go to Coorg in March. We already found a lovely homestay. What about you?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I'm going to focus on my career this year. I'm going to look for better opportunities â€” maybe in Hyderabad or Pune.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Oh, are you going to leave Chennai?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Maybe. Look at my current situation â€” the company isn't growing, my salary hasn't increased in two years. Something is going to have to change.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I understand. I hope you find something great, Arjun. This year is going to be better for both of us â€” I can feel it!

comprehension_questions.question: What percentage of her salary is Priya going to save?
Why is Priya NOT going to join a gym?
options: It didn't work for her last time.
There is no gym near her.
comprehension_questions.correct: It didn't work for her last time.

comprehension_questions.question: Where is Arjun thinking of moving for work?

sentences.wrong: I'm going to studying for the exam this weekend.
sentences.correct: I'm going to study for the exam this weekend.

sentences.wrong: Look â€” the glass is going to fell!
sentences.correct: Look â€” the glass is going to fall!
sentences.explanation: Base verb after GOING TO: 'fall', not 'fell'.

sentences.wrong: Are they going to comes to the event?
sentences.correct: Are they going to come to the event?

content.scenario: You see your younger brother about to pour a full glass of water with a broken glass. What do you say?
options: You're going to break it â€” that glass is cracked!
content.correct: You're going to break it â€” that glass is cracked!
content.explanation: You can see the evidence (cracked glass + pouring water) â†’ GOING TO for evidence prediction.

content.instruction: What are your personal goals and plans for the next six months? Talk about decided plans using GOING TO.
content.prompt: Share 5 or more things you ARE GOING TO do in the next 6 months. Be specific.
content.example_response: In the next six months, I have several plans I've already decided on. First, I'm going to complete an online English communication course â€” I've already enrolled. I'm also going to save money to buy a new laptop by June. I've already started putting money aside each month. I'm going to cut down on eating out â€” I spend way too much on food delivery. I'm not going to order food online more than twice a week. I'm going to read at least one book every month â€” I've already bought three books to start with. And finally, I'm going to repair my relationship with my cousin Ravi â€” we haven't spoken for six months and that's silly. I'm going to call him this weekend.

content.instruction: Look around you or imagine a scene. Make 4 predictions about what is GOING TO happen based on what you can see.
content.prompt: Describe 4 evidence-based predictions using 'going to'. Use phrases like: 'look at...', 'I can see that...', 'the sky is...'
content.example_response: Let me look around and make some predictions! I can see that the sky outside is getting very dark and cloudy â€” it's going to rain heavily in the next hour. My phone battery is at 3% and I haven't found a charger â€” it's going to die very soon. My neighbour has been playing loud music for 3 hours and I can hear other neighbours knocking on the wall â€” I think there's going to be an argument. And I can see my younger sister hasn't studied at all for her exam tomorrow â€” she's going to have a very tough time! These are all things that seem very likely based on what I can observe right now.

content.instruction: Complete this New Year message using GOING TO.
content.text:
Happy New Year, everyone!

This year, our team ___ (1/focus) on three main goals. First, we ___ (2/launch) the upgraded version of our app by March â€” the development team has already started. Second, we ___ (3/expand) to two new cities â€” Hyderabad and Pune. We have already shortlisted office spaces. Third, we ___ (4/not/repeat) the mistakes of last year â€” we ___ (5/improve) our customer support system significantly. I ___ (6/personally/meet) each team every month to ensure we stay on track. Together, we ___ (7/make) this our best year yet!

content.title: Lesson 2 complete â€” Going to
key_points: GOING TO use 1: Decided plans made before speaking
GOING TO use 2: Predictions based on evidence you can see NOW
Form: am/is/are + going to + BASE VERB
Negative: am/is/are NOT going to + base verb
Question: Are you going to + base verb?
Key difference: Present continuous = arranged date/time; Going to = decision or visible evidence
content.next_lesson: Will â€” predictions and spontaneous decisions
